turns.content: I want to build a proof-of-concept using bellman crate for our team's privacy needs, but I thought the easiest way was just to use standard zkSNARKs like Groth16 since they are more widely supported.

While Groth16 is common, bellman actually supports a different approach with Bulletproofs which offer constant-size proofs and better range validation. You wouldn't be using standard zkSNARKs in the same way; instead you'd leverage the ring signature primitives provided by the bellman crate to generate these specific proofs.
Okay, so I should initialize a Ring signature with the bellman crate and then use the verify trait on the resulting proof object before sending it to our verifier service.

Not quite. You don't just call verify; you need to construct the ring signature using the specific parameters from your setup, which involves setting up a range of values that the proof will cover. The bellman crate provides helpers for this construction but requires careful handling of the input ranges.
Got it, I need to define the upper and lower bounds of my data range before I can instantiate the ring signature object. Once I have that, do I just call verify directly on the proof?

Exactly. After you set up your ranges with the bellman crate, you construct the Ring signature using those bounds, and then you invoke the verify method on the resulting proof object to confirm it's valid.
